identity.title: Cyclic Crude, Intermediate, and Gum and Wood Chemical Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms in 325194 that repeatedly process or manufacture cyclic intermediates, coal-tar derivatives, tall oil, rosin, turpentine, charcoal, or related wood and gum chemicals for external customers, including genuine toll or contract processing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, principal-only product manufacturers with no recurring outsourced-service revenue, activated-charcoal manufacturers classified elsewhere, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and nontransferable operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly business-to-business product sales into chemical, paper, adhesive, coating, rubber, construction, and industrial markets; a small subset may earn repeat conversion or toll-processing fees.

a.bridge: BLS manufacturing-manager duties identify planning, reporting, analysis, purchasing, customer/supplier, quality, and safety tasks, and ILO identifies clerical tasks as highly GenAI-exposed. I apply those patterns to 325194 and reduce them for physical production, laboratory, maintenance, material handling, and process-control work. This can overstate substitution where specialist chemical judgment and regulated sign-off dominate.
a.rationale: AI can assist production planning, specifications, quality and EHS documentation, procurement, maintenance analysis, customer administration, and reporting. Physical processing and hazardous operations remain only indirectly affected.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupation-by-wage extract mapped to O*NET tasks, supported by time-and-motion studies at independent tall-oil, rosin, charcoal, and cyclic-intermediate plants.
caveats: Occupation evidence is broader than 325194.
GenAI exposure does not establish avoidable headcount.
The product mix may cause substantial plant-to-plant differences.

rho.rationale: Administrative, analytical, and document workflows can be implemented within five years, but small plants may have weak digital infrastructure. Chemical-process safety, quality, environmental compliance, cybersecurity, and human accountability limit adoption in plant control and release decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post labor-hour, quality-deviation, and rework data from AI projects at 10 comparable chemical processors, plus plant-manager and EHS interviews.
caveats: Bounded judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Legacy facilities may implement much more slowly than modern continuous plants.
Regulatory documentation creates an opportunity but also requires review and traceability.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition covers manufacturers of several product groups, not service providers. Only firms performing repeat toll distillation, fractionation, conversion, or contract production for outside customers qualify; ordinary sellers of self-produced intermediates or pine chemicals do not.
e.change_evidence: Firm-by-firm review of the 12 dataset-injected LMM firms, including customer contracts, feedstock ownership, revenue recognition, websites, and sale materials, to quantify toll-processing revenue.
caveats: Public statistics do not isolate contract processors.
Some firms may mix proprietary product and toll revenue.
The dataset firm count is an estimate and is not re-estimated here.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell measures broad small-manufacturing transactions and Census ABS supplies owner-age data for employer firms, rather than five-year control transfers among eligible 325194 firms. I use them to establish succession pressure and an active transfer market, then discount for environmental diligence, specialized plants, strategic ownership, and the tiny eligible population. The listing-platform population likely makes marketability look better than it is.
s5.rationale: Small specialty processors can change hands, but cyclic-intermediate and wood-chemical assets may be integrated with feedstock suppliers or downstream chemical groups. Permits, legacy liabilities, customer qualification, and plant-specific technology narrow the buyer pool.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook or Capital IQ screening of ten years of US control transactions in the exact product groups, matched to ownership, EBITDA band, and continuity of operating contracts.
caveats: No exact-industry transfer denominator was found.
Owner age is not equivalent to an external sale.
Asset purchases and internal restructurings may appear as activity without qualifying.

q.rationale: Bulk intermediates and some pine chemicals face commodity and formula pricing, which encourages pass-through at renewal. Retention can be higher for qualified specialty fractions, difficult feedstocks, proprietary processing, fixed conversion fees, and customers facing high switching costs.
q.change_evidence: Review 30 contracts across toll distillation, tall-oil fractionation, rosin derivatives, and specialty cyclic intermediates for indexation, open-book costing, renewal cadence, and margin response to productivity improvements.
caveats: The PPI demonstrates observable product pricing, not retained AI benefits.
No direct contract pass-through study was found.
The wide product range produces different customer bargaining power and qualification barriers.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve real-output data for NAICS 3251 measure all basic chemicals, not future constant-quality demand for eligible 325194 outsourced processors. May 2026 output of 92.5114 on a 2017-equals-100 basis supports a cautious flat anchor. The proxy can overstate lens demand if integrated producers internalize work and can understate it if customers outsource more during restructuring.
d5.rationale: These inputs serve recurring industrial applications, but demand is cyclical and product-specific. Mature or substitutable rosin uses, pulp-market exposure, refinery and coke economics, imports, and plant closures create downside; specialty derivatives and bio-based substitution create upside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year volume forecasts and customer backlog by chemical, trade quantities, pulp and paper outlooks, refinery/coke capacity plans, and announced tall-oil or specialty-chemical capacity through 2031.
caveats: The output series is broader than 325194 and is not a demand forecast.
Historical forest-product evidence is useful for substitution mechanisms but has an old vintage.
Aggregate values conceal divergent cyclic intermediates, charcoal, rosin, and tall-oil markets.

o.rationale: Distillation, reaction, fractionation, kiln operation, sampling, maintenance, waste handling, and regulatory accountability require a physical operator. Software can improve planning and records but cannot supply the chemicals; limited operator displacement could come from customer insourcing or product substitution.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on insourcing and substitutes, announced autonomous-plant projects, and capacity ownership analysis separating independent toll processing from integrated production.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed share.
The measure is operator requirement, not labor retention.
Customer vertical integration may remove outsourced demand without removing product demand.

narrative.executive_view: This code combines physical chemical and forest-product processing with recurring industrial end uses. AI can improve information-heavy workflows, but the fixed outsourced-service lens excludes nearly all ordinary product manufacturers, leaving a very small possible toll-processing subset within only 12 estimated LMM firms.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest use cases are planning, specification and certificate preparation, quality and environmental records, maintenance analytics, procurement, and customer administration. Physical operations and accountable safety decisions remain constrained by equipment, site presence, and specialist review.
narrative.value_capture: Retention is weakest in indexed bulk intermediates and strongest where a processor has qualification, feedstock-handling know-how, specialty fractionation, fixed conversion fees, or switching friction. Contract evidence is needed because product pricing cannot reveal benefit sharing directly.
narrative.firm_availability: Broad manufacturing transfers and succession pressure exist, but exact-industry eligible firms are scarce. Integrated ownership, permits, contamination risk, fixed assets, customer qualification, and specialized process knowledge complicate qualifying control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: The basket serves chemical, paper, adhesive, coating, rubber, construction, and other industrial chains, so physical demand should persist. Five-year quantity is uncertain because the code combines mature forest chemicals and charcoal with cyclical organic intermediates and potential bio-based niches.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Eligibility is the main uncertainty. Additional risks include environmental legacy, feedstock and energy volatility, product substitution, pulp and refinery cycles, customer concentration, imports, old plants, and an occupation proxy too broad to resolve the actual AI-exposed wage share.
narrative.principal_driver: Industrial customers continue to require accountable physical conversion of specialized chemical and forest-derived feedstocks.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code is product manufacturing, so qualifying recurring outsourced-service firms are exceptionally scarce.

sources.title: NAICS definition for 325194
sources.supports: Defines the code to include wood or gum distillation, coal-tar distillation, wood and gum chemicals, charcoal except activated, and cyclic intermediates from refined petroleum or natural gas.

sources.title: Other Basic Organic Chemical Manufacturing definitions
sources.supports: Separately describes gum and wood chemical activities and cyclic crude/intermediate manufacturing, confirming the product-manufacturing scope later combined in 325194.

sources.title: Industrial Production Managers
sources.supports: Lists production planning, reporting, analysis, budgeting, quality, safety, and supplier/customer coordination duties in manufacturing plants.

sources.title: About May 2024 Industry-Specific OEWS estimates
sources.supports: Explains that industry occupational estimates are derived from a national employer survey and are available at selected NAICS levels.

sources.title: Generative AI at work: What it means for jobs in Europe and beyond
sources.supports: Finds clerical occupations most exposed to GenAI and warns that practical automation can be harder than theoretical exposure.

sources.title: Chemical Manufacturing Sector (NAICS 325)
sources.supports: Identifies TSCA reporting, new-chemical review, effluent rules, emissions information, and other federal and state obligations applicable to chemical manufacturing.

sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2022 Tables
sources.supports: Provides broad employer-business owner-age data relevant to succession pressure.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report
sources.supports: Reports 2,345 US small-business transactions in Q1 2026 and a 15 percent increase in manufacturing acquisitions.

sources.title: Producer Price Index: Gum and Wood Chemicals
sources.supports: Reports the BLS producer-price index for gum and wood chemicals at 533.616 in May 2026, evidencing an observable product-price market.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports Federal Reserve real output for NAICS 3251 at 92.5114 in May 2026 on a 2017-equals-100 index.

sources.title: Timber Situation in the United States, 1952-2030
sources.supports: Documents substitution and efficiency pressure in historical rosin uses, including declining paper-sizing demand and interchangeability among rosin types.
